lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Post speedily to my lord your husband; show him this letter: the

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: army of France is landed. Seek out the villain Gloucester.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Hang him instantly.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Pluck out his eyes.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Leave him to my displeasure. Edmund, keep you our sister company: the

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: revenges we are bound to take upon your traitorous father are not

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: fit for your beholding. Advise the duke, where you are going, to

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: a most festinate preparation: we are bound to the like. Our posts

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: shall be swift and intelligent betwixt us. Farewell, dear sister: farewell, my

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: lord of Gloucester.

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: My lord of Gloucester hath convey’d him hence:

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: Some five or six and thirty of his knights,

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: Hot questrists after him, met him at gate;

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: Who, with some other of the lords dependants,

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: Are gone with him towards Dover; where they boast

lines.speaker: OSWALD
lines.text: To have well-armed friends.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Get horses for your mistress.

lines.speaker: GONERIL
lines.text: Farewell, sweet lord, and sister.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Edmund, farewell.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Go seek the traitor Gloucester,

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Pinion him like a thief, bring him before us.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Though well we may not pass upon his life

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Without the form of justice, yet our power

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Shall do a courtesy to our wrath, which men

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: May blame, but not control. Who’s there? the traitor?

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Ingrateful fox! ’tis he.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Bind fast his corky arms.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: What mean your graces? Good my friends, consider

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: You are my guests: do me no foul play, friends.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Bind him, I say. Servants bind him.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Hard, hard. O filthy traitor!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Unmerciful lady as you are, I’m none.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: To this chair bind him. Villain, thou shalt find—Regan plucks his beard.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: By the kind gods, ’tis most ignobly done

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To pluck me by the beard.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: So white, and such a traitor!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Naughty lady,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: These hairs, which thou dost ravish from my chin,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Will quicken, and accuse thee: I am your host:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: With robbers’ hands my hospitable favours

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: You should not ruffle thus. What will you do?

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Come, sir, what letters had you late from France?

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Be simple answerer, for we know the truth.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: And what confederacy have you with the traitors

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Late footed in the kingdom?

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: To whose hands have you sent the lunatic king? Speak.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I have a letter guessingly set down,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Which came from one that’s of a neutral heart,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And not from one opposed.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Cunning.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: And false.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Where hast thou sent the king?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To Dover.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Wherefore to Dover? Wast thou not charged at peril—

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Wherefore to Dover? Let him first answer that.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I am tied to the stake, and I must stand the course.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Wherefore to Dover, sir?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Because I would not see thy cruel nails

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Pluck out his poor old eyes; nor thy fierce sister

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: In his anointed flesh stick boarish fangs.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: The sea, with such a storm as his bare head

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: In hell-black night endured, would have buoy’d up,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And quench’d the stelled fires:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Yet, poor old heart, he holp the heavens to rain.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: If wolves had at thy gate howl’d that stern time,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Thou shouldst have said “Good porter, turn the key,”

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: All cruels else subscribed: but I shall see

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: The winged vengeance overtake such children.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: See’t shalt thou never. Fellows, hold the chair.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Upon these eyes of thine I’ll set my foot.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: He that will think to live till he be old,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Give me some help! O cruel! O you gods!

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: One side will mock another; the other too.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: If you see vengeance—

lines.speaker: FIRST SERVANT
lines.text: Hold your hand, my lord:

lines.speaker: FIRST SERVANT
lines.text: I have served you ever since I was a child;

lines.speaker: FIRST SERVANT
lines.text: But better service have I never done you

lines.speaker: FIRST SERVANT
lines.text: Than now to bid you hold.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: How now, you dog!

lines.speaker: FIRST SERVANT
lines.text: If you did wear a beard upon your chin,

lines.speaker: FIRST SERVANT
lines.text: I’d shake it on this quarrel. What do you mean?

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: My villain! They draw and fight.

lines.speaker: FIRST SERVANT
lines.text: Nay, then, come on, and take the chance of anger.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Give me thy sword. A peasant stand up thus!

lines.speaker: FIRST SERVANT
lines.text: O, I am slain! My lord, you have one eye left

lines.speaker: FIRST SERVANT
lines.text: To see some mischief on him. O!

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Lest it see more, prevent it. Out, vile jelly!

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Where is thy lustre now?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: All dark and comfortless. Where’s my son Edmund?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Edmund, enkindle all the sparks of nature,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To quit this horrid act.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Out, treacherous villain!

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Thou call’st on him that hates thee: it was he

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: That made the overture of thy treasons to us;

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Who is too good to pity thee.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: O my follies! then Edgar was abused.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Kind gods, forgive me that, and prosper him!

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: Go thrust him out at gates, and let him smell

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: His way to Dover.

lines.speaker: REGAN
lines.text: How is’t, my lord? how look you?

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: I have received a hurt: follow me, lady.

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Turn out that eyeless villain; throw this slave

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Upon the dunghill. Regan, I bleed apace:

lines.speaker: CORNWALL
lines.text: Untimely comes this hurt: give me your arm.

lines.speaker: SECOND SERVANT
lines.text: I’ll never care what wickedness I do,

lines.speaker: SECOND SERVANT
lines.text: If this man come to good.

lines.speaker: THIRD SERVANT
lines.text: If she live long,

lines.speaker: THIRD SERVANT
lines.text: And in the end meet the old course of death,

lines.speaker: THIRD SERVANT
lines.text: Women will all turn monsters.

lines.speaker: SECOND SERVANT
lines.text: Let’s follow the old earl, and get the Bedlam

lines.speaker: SECOND SERVANT
lines.text: To lead him where he would: his roguish madness

lines.speaker: SECOND SERVANT
lines.text: Allows itself to any thing.

lines.speaker: THIRD SERVANT
lines.text: Go thou: I’ll fetch some flax and whites of eggs

lines.speaker: THIRD SERVANT
lines.text: To apply to his bleeding face. Now, heaven help him!

stage_directions.text: Enter Cornwall, Regan, Goneril, Edmund, and Servants.
Enter Gloucester, brought in by two or three.
Takes a sword, and runs at him behind.

glosses.definition: ride with all haste
addressing Edmund — who now holds the title
Goneril's chilling suggestion for Gloucester's punishment — the play's most disturbing line uttered casually
we are bound to the law of courtesy
dry, withered old arms — bindings for Gloucester
Gloucester's flat denial under interrogation
Cornwall's epithet for Gloucester
rumple; disarrange — of his beard being pulled
conspiracy
face the consequences
the king's body, consecrated by coronation — a direct appeal to divine monarchy
swift divine retribution

glosses.phrase: Upon these eyes of thine I’ll set my foot
glosses.definition: Cornwall's explicit threat, immediately followed by the act
Cornwall's vow — Gloucester shall never see again
Cornwall gouging out Gloucester's eye — one of the most shocking lines in Jacobean theater, staged onstage rather than offstage
Cornwall's taunt to the just-blinded eye
natural; proper
the First Servant's intervention — a tiny moral resistance in a scene of cruelty
peasant — Cornwall's outrage that a servant would stand against him

glosses.phrase: Lest it see more, prevent it
glosses.definition: Cornwall to the second eye — completing the blinding
Cornwall's line repeated for the second eye — the doubling is pointed
Gloucester in blind despair
Gloucester naming Edmund as betrayer — the moment he understands
Gloucester's anagnorisis — recognition of his own blindness in trusting Edmund
his true son was wronged

glosses.phrase: Go thrust him out at gates
glosses.definition: Regan orders Gloucester thrown out of his own castle
Regan's grotesque final cruelty — a blind man finding Dover by smell
eager hunters pursuing Lear
Cornwall's label for Gloucester
Regan dismissing Lear
a bear-baiting term — the chained victim's 'course' against dogs

glosses.phrase: the sea, with such a storm
glosses.definition: Gloucester's account of Lear's condition in the storm
all cruelties personified
in the darkness of hell

glosses.phrase: if wolves had at thy gate howl’d
glosses.definition: even wolves would have shown more mercy
swift divine retribution
insult — Cornwall calls the servant a peasant
bind his arms — bracketing Gloucester before the torture
wicked woman — Gloucester to Regan; 'naughty' is Jacobean severe, not modern mild
one who owes the duty of hospitality — the very category Cornwall and Regan are violating
risen up to cover
folk remedy for eye-injury; Regan's contemptuous dismissal of treatment
the eye reduced to biological substrate — the line's shock
Cornwall, wounded, recognises his injury

annotations.title: 'Out, vile jelly!' — the play's deliberate shock
annotations.body: Cornwall's putting out of Gloucester's eyes, onstage, was shocking even by Jacobean standards. Earlier tragedies happen at a distance; here the violence is staged, with Gloucester bound, Cornwall's thumb described putting pressure on the eye. Nineteenth-century productions routinely cut or offstaged the blinding; Nahum Tate's 1681 adaptation simply omitted it. Shakespeare's deliberate choice to put the act in front of the audience is the play's moral argument made theatrical. Gloucester, who 'had eyes' metaphorically — who trusted what he could see and was therefore deceived by Edmund's forged letter — must literally lose them to perceive what his metaphorical sight missed. The 'Out, vile jelly!' line concentrates the play's horror into four words; 'jelly' reduces the eye to its biological substrate. Victorian audiences found this unbearable; modern audiences should not find it less so. The scene's purpose is to be unbearable.
sources: Jonathan Bate, Soul of the Age (2008)

annotations.title: The servant who intervenes — a tiny act of moral resistance
annotations.body: In the middle of Gloucester's blinding, an unnamed First Servant — a household retainer — draws his sword on Cornwall: 'Hold your hand, my lord. / I have serv'd you ever since I was a child, / But better service have I never done you / Than now to bid you hold.' He wounds Cornwall fatally (the injury will kill Cornwall offstage, reported in 4.2). Regan snatches a sword and kills the servant from behind. The moment is easy to miss because it happens inside the scene's larger atrocity, but it is structurally crucial: the play's only direct onstage resistance to the sisters' cruelty comes not from a nobleman but from a serving-man, anonymous, who pays for it with his life. Shakespeare gives him no name because the point is that moral resistance can come from anywhere — and that in this play it is always punished.

annotations.title: Gloucester's recognition — 'O my follies!'
annotations.body: Blinded, Gloucester hears from Regan that 'it was he / That made the overture of thy treasons to us' — Edmund. The instant recognition: 'O my follies! then Edgar was abused. / Kind gods, forgive me that, and prosper him!' Gloucester achieves full understanding only after losing his eyes — the play's most ruthless formalization of its blindness-as-insight theme. Shakespeare makes the anagnorisis minimalist. Gloucester does not catalogue his errors; he names Edgar, asks forgiveness, hopes for his son's prospering. There is nothing left to do. The gods he invokes will not answer — 4.1's 'As flies to wanton boys' comes directly from this Gloucester, now fully disabused.

trials.prompt: Why does Shakespeare stage Gloucester's blinding onstage rather than reporting it?
options: The play's argument requires the shock — Gloucester who 'had eyes' metaphorically must lose them literally to perceive what his metaphorical sight missed; the scene's unbearability is its moral point
Shakespeare wanted the play to be shorter

trials.prompt: What is the significance of the unnamed First Servant who wounds Cornwall?
options: He is a minor comic figure

The play's only direct onstage resistance to the sisters' cruelty — and it comes from an anonymous serving-man who dies for it; Shakespeare making moral resistance class-mobile and immediately punished
He is actually Kent in disguise

trials.prompt: What does Gloucester recognise immediately after being blinded?
options: That Edmund has betrayed him and Edgar was innocent — 'O my follies! then Edgar was abused' — full moral sight arrives only after physical blindness

trials.prompt: What did Nahum Tate's 1681 adaptation of Lear do with the blinding scene?
options: He omitted it entirely — along with the tragic ending; Tate's Lear (in which Cordelia lives and marries Edgar) was the standard performance text for 150 years
He moved it earlier in the play
